Written c. 180 BC during the Hellenistic period, emphasizing traditional Jewish values against Greek influence.
Reflects the trials of a community under foreign rule, looking back to the generations of the Exodus and Patriarchs for strength.
Reinforces the core commandment of the Decalogue (Honoring Parents) within a Second Temple social framework.
Reflects the economic disparities of Jerusalem in the 2nd Century BC and the Jewish tradition of charity.
Addresses the temptations of wealth and political security during the period of Seleucid administration.
Mirrors the educational traditions of the Jewish scribe (Sofer) in late antiquity.
Documents the social structure of the time, including the high status and support required for the Aaronic priesthood.
Provides insight into the political caution required when dealing with mighty men (likely Seleucid officials).
Illustrates the moral challenges and social life (singing, banquets) of urban Hellenistic Jerusalem.
Explains the translation of kingdoms through the lens of God's sovereignty, relevant to the rise and fall of Near Eastern empires.
Reflects the precarious nature of social status in the Hellenistic world, where fortunes changed rapidly.
Highlights the sectarian tension and distrust of those deemed sinners or enemies in late Second Temple Judaism.
A critique of the social stratification between the urban elite and the rural or pious poor in Judea.
Addresses the Jewish concept of Sheol (the grave) as a place where the dead do not seek dainties.
A philosophical defense of the Torah and free will, potentially countering Hellenistic fatalism or Stoicism.
Uses Old Testament narratives (Giants, Lot, Pharaoh) to establish the continuity of God's judgment.
Connects the creation of man with the specific heritage given to Israel as the Lord's portion.
Reflects Second Temple theology regarding the vast difference between the Eternal Creator and mortal man.
Critiques the behaviors common in Hellenistic banquets and the risks of unchecked information exchange.
Provides a window into the rhetorical training and social expectations of a wise Jew in the 2nd century BC.
Reflects the architectural features (stairs, gates) and social settings of ancient Jerusalem.
Highlights the high value placed on family honor and the protection of the paternal estate in Jewish society.
Addresses the ritual and legal concerns regarding swearing and the sanctity of marriage in Jewish law.
Key text for understanding Wisdom theology (Sapiential) which later influenced New Testament Christology.
Reflects the traditional social observations of the elders in a communal Jewish culture.
Documents gender roles and domestic expectations in pre-Maccabean Israel.
Provides historical insight into the ethics of trade and the marketplace in an urban center like Jerusalem.
Reflects the moral urgency of communal harmony during times of foreign political pressure.
Outlines the practical application of Mosaic law regarding loans and charity in a 2nd century context.
Documents child-rearing practices and the high value of paternal authority in ancient Judaism.
Insights into the diet and social customs of Jewish banquets, including the consumption of wine.
Illustrates the protocol and social hierarchies at formal Jewish meals.
Uses the potter metaphor (common in Jeremiah and Isaiah) to explain divine providence during the intertestamental period.
Addresses the popular but potentially misleading role of dreams in the religious life of the time.
Emphasizes the prophetic priority of justice over empty ritual, a core theme of Jewish piety.
A prayer expressing the nationalistic hope for the restoration of the twelve tribes and the end of foreign rule.
Highlights the importance of the advisor and the scribe in the Hellenistic Jewish educational system.
One of the few ancient texts to explicitly reconcile the religious role of God with the professional role of the physician.
Defines the ideal Scribe as a scholar of the Law, contrasting with manual laborers and craftsmen.
Reflects the universal human experience of labor and anxiety, grounded in the Jewish understanding of the Fall.
Reflects the societal importance of legacy and name before the widespread development of belief in personal resurrection.
Reflects the patriarchal concerns regarding daughters and the oversight of a household.
Uses the terminology of contemporary astronomy to describe the sun, moon, and the seasons within a monotheistic framework.
A panegyric of the fathers, reinforcing Jewish identity through a summarized history of the lineage from Enoch to the Patriarchs.
Strong emphasis on the legitimacy and glory of the Zadokite/Aaronic priesthood, central to Second Temple life.
Recalls the period of the Conquest and the early Judges as the foundation of the nation's strength.
Analyzes the United Monarchy, praising the establishment of the temple and Davidic dynasty while mourning its later split.
Recounts the Northern prophets and the miraculous deliverance of Jerusalem under Hezekiah.
Celebrates the last reformers and the early Persian-period restoration figures like Nehemiah.
Likely refers to Simon II (the Just), providing a rare contemporary description of temple ritual and the High Priest's status.
The author (Jesus son of Sirach) provides a personal conclusion, detailing his own training and the school (Beth Midrash) he founded.
Set during the Babylonian Captivity, illustrating the legal struggles and social life of the Jewish Diaspora in Babylon.
Reflects the Assyrian deportation of the Northern Kingdom (Naphtali) in the 8th Century BC.
Illustrates the dangers and ritual requirements for Jews living under Sennacherib and Esarhaddon.
Provides a window into Jewish life in Media (Ecbatana) during the Diaspora.
Reflects the sapiential (wisdom) traditions common in Second Temple Jewish literature.
Shows the geography of the ancient Near East and the role of professional guides in travel.
Reflects folk-medicine and beliefs about the spiritual properties of natural objects in antiquity.
Documents ancient Jewish marriage customs and the importance of tribal endogamy.
Combines ritual exorcism with liturgical prayer in a domestic setting.
Highlights the importance of social and financial trust (suretyship) within the Diaspora community.
Portrays the emotional life of Jewish parents in the Diaspora and the duty of children to return.
The restoration of sight as a sign of divine favor, a common motif in scripture.
Develops the angelology of the Second Temple period, specifically the role of intercessory angels.
Shares the exilic hope for the gathering of Israel and the beautification of the Temple.
Anticipates the fall of Nineveh (612 BC) and the subsequent Persian period restoration.
Reflects the Jewish Hellenistic environment of Alexandria (c. 1st Century BC) addressing universal ethics.
Likely responds to Epicurean or skeptical Greek philosophies prevalent in the Hellenistic period.
Addresses the reality of Jewish martyrdom and persecution under foreign rule.
Reflects the common Hellenistic concern with the meaning of a short life vs. a long life.
Uses military imagery common in the Roman and Hellenistic eras to describe God's intervention.
A Mirror for Princes text, common in ancient literature, advising rulers on virtue.
Utilizes the persona of Solomon, the archetype of wisdom in the Jewish tradition.
Adapts Greek philosophical terms (the four cardinal virtues: temperance, prudence, justice, fortitude).
Reflects the dualistic thought (corruptible body vs. soul) common in Platonism, filtered through Jewish faith.
A Midrashic retelling of Genesis and Exodus narratives.
Reflects Jewish theological attempts to reconcile the plagues of Egypt with a merciful God.
Explains the Conquest of Canaan in a way that emphasizes God's justice and opportunity for repentance.
A classic Jewish polemic against Hellenistic and Egyptian polytheism.
Links the history of idolatry to the divinization of dead relatives and kings (Euhemerism).
Critiques the Egyptian practice of animal worship and the manufacturing of idols.
Reflects the Jewish liturgical and educational focus on the Exodus events.
A psychological interpretation of the ninth plague of Egypt.
Portrays the Word (Logos) as an active agent, bridge-building toward later New Testament concepts.
Summarizes the Exodus as a new creation, where the elements are rearranged for Israel's sake.
Written by Paul from Ephesus (c. AD 55) to the church in Corinth, a major Greek port city known for wealth, diversity, and moral laxity.
Reflects the Greek cultural emphasis on sophia (wisdom) and rhetoric. Paul contrasts this with the foolishness of the gospel.
The Corinthian church members were behaving as babes in Christ, distracted by personalities rather than focusing on spiritual growth.
Apostles in the 1st century were often despised and persecuted. Paul challenges the Corinthians' feelings of spiritual self-sufficiency.
Incest was considered a crime even by pagan standards in the Roman world. The church's failure to act showed a dangerous lack of spiritual boundaries.
Corinthians were litigious. Paul's instruction regarding Saints judging the world reflects the LDS doctrine of the exaltation of man.
Addresses questions sent by the Corinthians. Paul's preference for celibacy relates to the present distress (persecution) and missionary needs.
In Corinth, meat sold in markets often came from animals sacrificed in pagan temples, creating a moral dilemma for converts.
Paul highlights the custom of supporting traveling ministers. His refusal to take pay was strategic to avoid accusations of preaching for money.
References the Exodus journey and the temptations Israel faced. Corinth's highly idolatrous environment made warnings about communion with devils relevant.
Customs regarding hair and head coverings were significant social markers in 1st-century Greco-Roman society. The Lord's Supper had devolved into a social feast.
The Corinthians were over-emphasizing extravagant gifts like tongues. Paul corrects this by stressing the organic unity of the church.
Written as a corrective to the Corinthians' pride over spiritual gifts. Charity is presented as the essential motivation for all Christian service.
Early Christian meetings were participatory and occasionally chaotic. Paul establishes protocols to ensure edification rather than confusion.
Responds to some who were denying the physical resurrection, a common view in certain Greek philosophical circles. Paul's mention of baptism for the dead is a vital LDS proof-text.
The collection for the saints refers to Paul's major project of gathering relief funds from Gentile churches for the impoverished mother church in Jerusalem.
John writes from Ephesus to combat Docetic Gnosticism, which denied Christ's physical body.
The church was facing internal apostasy from those who had left and were now teaching false doctrines.
John encourages Saints to maintain their identity as children of God despite growing worldly pressure.
Discernment was critical as various teachers claimed spiritual authority to lead the Saints astray.
John uses legal terminology regarding witnesses to establish the absolute truth of the Christian record.
Peter addresses the strangers scattered across various Roman provinces in Asia Minor (modern-day Turkey).
The imagery of lively stones highlights the early Christian shift from physical Jewish temple worship to a communal spiritual identity.
Peter's mention of spirits in prison provides an apostolic foundation for Christ's ministry between His death and resurrection.
The fiery trial mentioned likely refers to localized persecutions of Christians under the Roman Empire.
Peter's reference to Babylon is widely understood as a code name for Rome, where he likely composed the letter.
Written by Paul from Corinth to the Saints in Thessalonica.
Written by Paul from Corinth to the Saints in Thessalonica.
Written by Paul from Corinth to the Saints in Thessalonica.
Written by Paul from Corinth to the Saints in Thessalonica.
Written by Paul from Corinth to the Saints in Thessalonica.
Paul writing to Timothy who is overseeing the church in Ephesus.
Guidance on communal prayer and conduct within the early Christian assemblies.
Establishment of formal qualifications for local church officers (Bishops and Deacons).
A warning against early gnostic-like tendencies regarding asceticism.
Refinement of the church's internal welfare system and protocols for elders.
Counsel for Christians navigating social structures and wealth in the Roman Empire.
Paul writing from Macedonia to the church in Corinth after a period of severe trials in Asia.
Paul explains his change of plans and addresses the outcome of a previous disciplinary matter in Corinth.
Paul addresses the contrast between the waning Law of Moses and the enduring glory of the Gospel.
Paul defends the sincerity of his ministry against critics while acknowledging his physical frailty.
Paul articulates the heavenly motivations for his missionary labors and the reality of the post-mortal life.
Paul details the hardships he has endured to prove his faithfulness as a minister of God.
Titus joins Paul in Macedonia with encouraging news of the Corinthians' positive response to Paul's letters.
Paul encourages the collection for the poor Saints in Jerusalem, using the Macedonian churches as a model.
Paul finalizes instructions for the relief fund, emphasizing the spiritual importance of generosity.
Paul directly addresses challenges to his apostolic authority by super-apostles in Corinth.
Paul lists his apostolic credentials through his sufferings, illustrating the cost of his devotion.
Paul refers to visions given fourteen years prior to validate his authority and describes his constant spiritual refining.
Paul prepares for his third visit to Corinth, warning those who continue in sin and exhorting the faithful.
Written by John, likely from Ephesus, to the elect lady to strengthen them against growing gnostic-style heresies.
Peter writing from Rome shortly before his martyrdom.
Peter writing from Rome shortly before his martyrdom.
Peter writing from Rome shortly before his martyrdom.
Written by Paul from Corinth shortly after his first letter to address ongoing persecutions and doctrinal confusion.
Addressing a specific misunderstanding among the Saints that the day of Christ had already arrived or was immediately imminent.
Paul provides final behavioral corrections to a community where some members had stopped working in anticipation of the Second Coming.
A personal letter from John the Apostle in Ephesus to a faithful member named Gaius, addressing local church tensions and the support of missionaries.
Written by Paul from a Roman prison during his second and final imprisonment.
Paul writes as an aged apostle nearing the end of his life, passing leadership responsibilities to Timothy.
Reflects the growing pressure of Roman persecution and the internal threat of false teachings within the early church.
Written shortly before Paul's martyrdom, likely under Emperor Nero, marking his final recorded words.
Set in Jerusalem shortly after the Resurrection; highlights the geographical starting point for the world-wide mission.
The Feast of Pentecost was a major Jewish pilgrimage festival, drawing Jews from across the Roman Empire to Jerusalem.
The Beautiful gate and Solomon's porch were prominent locations within the second Temple complex.
Describes the early conflict between the Nazarenes and the Sadducean leadership of the Sanhedrin.
Gamaliel was a renowned Pharisaic teacher and member of the Sanhedrin, respected for his wisdom and moderation.
The tension between Grecians (Hellenistic Jews) and Hebrews (Judean Jews) within the early Christian community.
Stephen's discourse is the longest in Acts and summarizes the standard prophetic critique of Israel's history.
Samaria had been a center of religious tension with Jerusalem for centuries; the Ethiopian eunuch represents the outer reach of the known world.
The road to Damascus was a major trade route connecting Judea with the provinces of the North.
Caesarea was the Roman administrative capital of Judea and home to the Italian cohort.
Antioch was the third largest city in the Roman Empire and became the base for the Gentile mission.
Herod Agrippa I ruled Judea AD 41-44; his death is also recorded by the historian Josephus.
The practice of setting apart by the laying on of hands for specific missions is established here.
Lystra was a Roman colony in the province of Galatia; the locals worshipped Zeus and Hermes.
The Jerusalem Council (c. AD 49) was the first major internal policy crisis of the Christian church.
Philippi was a leading city of Macedonia and a Roman colony, enjoying special status under Roman law.
Athens was the intellectual capital of the ancient world; the Areopagus was its most prestigious judicial council.
Corinth was a major commercial hub; the mention of Gallio (proconsul AD 51-52) provides a firm date for Paul's visit.
Ephesus was home to the Temple of Artemis (Diana), one of the seven wonders of the ancient world.
Paul's journey reflects the end of his third missionary journey as he rushes to reach Jerusalem for Pentecost.
Jerusalem was under high tension due to Zealot activity and Roman oppression during the late 50s AD.
Roman citizenship conferred specific legal protections, including the right to a trial and immunity from scourging without conviction.
The Sanhedrin was the supreme Jewish court, but under Roman rule, its power to execute capital punishment was limited.
Antonius Felix was the Roman procurator of Judea (c. AD 52-59); he was known for being both effective and corrupt.
Porcius Festus succeeded Felix; the appeal to Caesar meant the case would be heard in Rome by the Emperor's court.
Herod Agrippa II was the last of the Herodian rulers; he had oversight of the Temple and a deep knowledge of Jewish law.
Provides a detailed account of ancient Mediterranean seafaring and the dangers of the autumn Euroclydon winds.
Rome was the capital of the Empire; Paul lived in his own hired house under house arrest for two years.
Written from prison (likely Rome) to the church at Colossae, a small city in the Lycus Valley of Asia Minor.
Paul addresses the Colossian heresy — a syncretic blend of Jewish legalism, pagan philosophy, and proto-gnostic asceticism threatening the young church.
Practical instruction for Gentile converts navigating new Christian ethics within Greco-Roman household structures.
Paul coordinates ministry from prison, connecting the Colossian, Laodicean, and broader Asian churches through shared messengers.
Written from prison (likely Rome) as a circular letter to the church at Ephesus and possibly other churches in Asia Minor.
Paul addresses a mixed congregation of Jewish and Gentile believers navigating their new shared identity in Christ.
Paul writes from imprisonment, reflecting on his unique calling to reveal the mystery of Gentile inclusion in God's plan.
Paul addresses the organizational structure of the early church and the challenge of maintaining unity amid diverse spiritual gifts.
Paul addresses Greco-Roman cultural norms around marriage and morality, reframing them through the lens of Christ's relationship with the church.
Paul uses Roman military imagery familiar to his audience to describe spiritual preparedness in a hostile pagan environment.
Paul writes urgently to the churches of Galatia (central Asia Minor) where Judaizing teachers are compelling Gentile converts to adopt Mosaic law.
Paul recounts events at the Jerusalem Council and the subsequent incident at Antioch where Peter withdrew from Gentile fellowship under pressure.
Paul engages Jewish scriptural arguments about the Abrahamic covenant and the temporary role of the Mosaic law in salvation history.
Paul uses rabbinic-style allegory of Hagar and Sarah, familiar to his opponents, to argue for freedom from the law.
Paul addresses the practical question facing Galatian converts: what does ethical living look like without the law as a guide?
Paul closes with personal remarks, writing in his own hand (large letters), emphasizing the cross over circumcision.
Written to Jewish converts facing intense pressure to return to traditional Judaism and the Law of Moses.
The author addresses the great salvation first spoken by the Lord and confirmed by those who heard Him.
Refers to the provocation in the wilderness, citing the forty-year wandering of Israel after the Exodus.
Draws on the Genesis Sabbath and Israel's failure to enter Canaan as a type of spiritual rest.
Refers to the established Jewish order of the High Priest (descended from Aaron) to contrast it with Christ.
Reflects a time when early Saints were tempted to turn away from the enlightenment they had received.
Examines the meeting between Abraham and Melchizedek in Genesis 14, justifying a priesthood outside of Levi.
Quotes extensively from Jeremiah 31, the primary Old Testament prophecy of a New Covenant.
Provides a detailed description of the Second Temple (or Tabernacle) layout, including the Holy of Holies.
Likely written before AD 70, as it speaks of sacrifices being offered year by year in the present tense.
A survey of Israelite history designed to bolster the courage of persecuted Christians.
Contrasts the terrifying physical manifestations at Mount Sinai with the spiritual peace of the New Jerusalem.
Mentions the liberation of Timothy and sends greetings from they of Italy, suggesting a Roman context.
Written to Christian converts of Jewish descent (the twelve tribes) living in the Diaspora, likely in the mid-1st century AD.
Addresses early Christian congregations struggling with socio-economic class distinctions.
Reflects the internal community dynamics and the significant influence of teachers and leaders in the early Church.
Deals with internal disputes and the influence of Hellenistic worldly values on the early Christian communities.
Provides instructions on the use of consecrated oil and the role of elders (priesthood) in the healing of the sick.
